expectations.text: It recommends AnyIO task groups instead of raw `asyncio.create_task()` for owned child work.
expectations.evidence: Line 11: 'Replace asyncio.create_task() with anyio.create_task_group() and tg.start_soon() / tg.start()'

expectations.text: It recommends memory object streams over raw `asyncio.Queue()` for new portable designs.
It replaces `asyncio.wait_for()` with AnyIO timeout/cancel-scope guidance rather than leaving it untouched.
expectations.evidence: Line 13: 'Replace asyncio.wait_for() with anyio.fail_after() or anyio.move_on_after()' and line 126: 'await asyncio.wait_for(coro, t) -> with fail_after(t): await coro'

expectations.text: It explicitly states that tasks spawned by native backend libraries on backends other than Trio are not subject to AnyIO's cancellation rules.
expectations.evidence: Lines 58-59: 'native tasks are not governed by AnyIO's structured cancellation semantics the same way' and lines 99-101: 'tasks spawned internally by an asyncio SDK are not under your task group's ownership... they may outlive your scope, ignore your shutdown timing, or surface errors differently'

expectations.text: It refuses to call the library fully portable while it still depends on an asyncio-native SDK that spawns its own tasks.
expectations.evidence: Lines 50-54: 'If one part of the library calls an asyncio-native vendor SDK that spawns its own tasks, then that part is not honestly Trio-portable. Not "hard to port." Not "portable with caveats." Not portable.' and lines 132-134: 'The worst assumption is: "If we wrap our own code in AnyIO, the asyncio vendor SDK becomes portable too." That assumption is false.'

expectations.text: It requires verification on both asyncio and Trio rather than a single-backend test run.
expectations.evidence: Lines 112-113: 'you need to run the backend-neutral test suite under both asyncio and Trio' and line 141: 'only claim full Trio support for the parts with no asyncio-native dependency path'
